<div className="sb-intro-page">

# Understanding the Theme System

<p className="lead-text">
  The YaleSites component library uses a three-tier theme system that works
  together to create cohesive, flexible, and branded experiences. Understanding
  how these three types of themes interact is key to effectively using the
  design system.
</p>

<div style={{ margin: '2rem 0' }}>
  <h3 style={{ marginBottom: '0.75rem', color: '#333' }}>Three Theme Types Working Together</h3>
  <p style={{ marginBottom: '1.5rem', color: '#444', lineHeight: '1.6' }}>
    Think of themes as layers: <strong>Global Theme</strong> sets the overall color palette,
    <strong> Section Theme</strong> applies background colors to page sections, and
    <strong> Component Theme (dial)</strong> adds accent colors to individual components.
    Together, they create unlimited design combinations while maintaining brand consistency.
  </p>
  <div style={{ position: 'relative', fontFamily: '-apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, "Segoe UI", Roboto, sans-serif' }}>

    {/* Global Theme - outermost layer */}
    <div style={{
      border: '3px solid #0053A0',
      borderRadius: '12px',
      padding: '1.5rem',
      background: '#e8f1f9',
      position: 'relative',
    }}>
      <div style={{
        display: 'flex',
        alignItems: 'center',
        gap: '0.75rem',
        marginBottom: '0.5rem',
      }}>
        <div style={{
          background: '#0053A0',
          color: 'white',
          padding: '0.25rem 0.75rem',
          borderRadius: '4px',
          fontSize: '0.75rem',
          fontWeight: '700',
          letterSpacing: '0.05em',
          textTransform: 'uppercase',
          whiteSpace: 'nowrap',
        }}>
          Global Theme (Lever)
        </div>
        <span style={{ fontSize: '0.9rem', color: '#333' }}>
          Sets the <strong>site-wide color palette</strong> — affects everything
        </span>
      </div>
      <div style={{ fontSize: '0.8rem', color: '#555', marginBottom: '1rem', paddingLeft: '0.25rem' }}>
        e.g. "Old Blues", "New Haven Green", "Shoreline Summer" …
      </div>

      {/* Section Theme - middle layer */}
      <div style={{
        border: '2px solid #2e7d32',
        borderRadius: '8px',
        padding: '1.25rem',
        background: '#eef5ee',
        position: 'relative',
      }}>
        <div style={{
          display: 'flex',
          alignItems: 'center',
          gap: '0.75rem',
          marginBottom: '0.5rem',
        }}>
          <div style={{
            background: '#2e7d32',
            color: 'white',
            padding: '0.25rem 0.75rem',
            borderRadius: '4px',
            fontSize: '0.75rem',
            fontWeight: '700',
            letterSpacing: '0.05em',
            textTransform: 'uppercase',
            whiteSpace: 'nowrap',
          }}>
            Section Theme
          </div>
          <span style={{ fontSize: '0.9rem', color: '#333' }}>
            Sets the <strong>background color of a page section</strong>
          </span>
        </div>
        <div style={{ fontSize: '0.8rem', color: '#555', marginBottom: '1rem', paddingLeft: '0.25rem' }}>
          e.g. default (white), one, two, three, four, five
        </div>

        {/* Component Theme - innermost layer */}
        <div style={{
          border: '2px solid #b35c00',
          borderRadius: '6px',
          padding: '1rem 1.25rem',
          background: '#fff8f0',
          position: 'relative',
        }}>
          <div style={{
            display: 'flex',
            alignItems: 'center',
            gap: '0.75rem',
            marginBottom: '0.5rem',
          }}>
            <div style={{
              background: '#b35c00',
              color: 'white',
              padding: '0.25rem 0.75rem',
              borderRadius: '4px',
              fontSize: '0.75rem',
              fontWeight: '700',
              letterSpacing: '0.05em',
              textTransform: 'uppercase',
              whiteSpace: 'nowrap',
            }}>
              Component Theme (Dial)
            </div>
            <span style={{ fontSize: '0.9rem', color: '#333' }}>
              Adds an <strong>accent color to an individual component</strong>
            </span>
          </div>
          <div style={{ fontSize: '0.8rem', color: '#555', paddingLeft: '0.25rem' }}>
            e.g. default, one, two, three, four, five — overrides only this component
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>

    {/* Legend */}
    <div style={{
      display: 'flex',
      gap: '2rem',
      marginTop: '1.25rem',
      flexWrap: 'wrap',
      fontSize: '0.85rem',
      color: '#555',
    }}>
      <div style={{ display: 'flex', alignItems: 'center', gap: '0.5rem' }}>
        <div style={{ width: '16px', height: '16px', background: '#0053A0', borderRadius: '3px' }} />
        Broadest scope — entire site
      </div>
      <div style={{ display: 'flex', alignItems: 'center', gap: '0.5rem' }}>
        <div style={{ width: '16px', height: '16px', background: '#2e7d32', borderRadius: '3px' }} />
        Mid scope — one page section
      </div>
      <div style={{ display: 'flex', alignItems: 'center', gap: '0.5rem' }}>
        <div style={{ width: '16px', height: '16px', background: '#b35c00', borderRadius: '3px' }} />
        Narrowest scope — one component
      </div>
    </div>

  </div>
</div>

---

## The Three Theme Types

### 1. Global Theme (Lever)

_Site-wide color palette_

The global theme is the foundation of your entire site's color scheme. It's called a "lever" because switching it fundamentally changes all the color values used throughout the site.

**Available options (6):**

- `one` - Old Blues
- `two` - New Haven Green
- `three` - Shoreline Summer
- `four` - Onha
- `five` - It's Your Yale
- `six` - AI

**How to control:**

- In Storybook: Use the "Site: Global Theme" dropdown in the top toolbar
- In Drupal: Set site-wide in theme settings

**What it affects:**

- All color tokens throughout the site
- Default colors for components that don't have specific theme overrides
- Base palette for section themes and component themes

**Think of it as:** The color filter applied to your entire website

---

### 2. Section Theme

_Background colors for page sections_

Section themes apply background colors to major page sections (like header, main content, footer). They work within the global theme's palette.

**Available options (6):**

- `default` - White/light background (most common)
- `one` through `five` - Colored backgrounds using global theme palette

**How to control:**

- In Storybook: Use the "Section Theme" control in the Controls panel for individual stories
- In Drupal: Set per-section in layout builder or paragraph settings

**What it affects:**

- Background color of page sections
- Automatically adjusts text color for contrast
- Provides context for components within the section

**Think of it as:** The canvas color for a section of your page

---

### 3. Component Theme (Dial)

_Accent colors for individual components_

Component themes (often called "dials" in the CMS) add accent colors to specific components. These are the most granular level of theming.

**Available options (6):**

- `default` - Uses global theme defaults
- `one` through `five` - Accent colors from global theme palette

**How to control:**

- In Storybook: Use the "[Component] Theme (dial)" control in the Controls panel
- In Drupal: Set per-component using the "Style" field (color dial)

**What it affects:**

- Component-specific accent colors (borders, backgrounds, highlights)
- Links and interactive elements within the component
- Component-specific decorative elements

**Think of it as:** The accent color for a specific component

---

## How They Work Together

The three theme types create a hierarchy:

1. **Global Theme** sets the base palette (e.g., "Old Blues")
2. **Section Theme** applies a background color to a page section (e.g., `two` = navy background)
3. **Component Theme** adds an accent color to a specific component (e.g., `three` = bright blue accent)

**Example combination:**

```
Global Theme: "New Haven Green" (site-wide)
└─ Section Theme: "two" (green background section)
   └─ Component Theme: "three" (bright accent on a card within that section)
```

This creates visual hierarchy and interest while maintaining brand consistency.

---

## Key Differences Explained

<table>
  <thead>
    <tr>
      <th>Theme Type</th>
      <th>Scope</th>
      <th>Controls</th>
      <th>Changes</th>
    </tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr>
      <td>
        <strong>Global Theme</strong>
      </td>
      <td>Entire site</td>
      <td>Color palette</td>
      <td>Affects all colors site-wide</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        <strong>Section Theme</strong>
      </td>
      <td>Page section</td>
      <td>Background color</td>
      <td>Affects one section background</td>
    </tr>
    <tr>
      <td>
        <strong>Component Theme</strong>
      </td>
      <td>Individual component</td>
      <td>Accent color</td>
      <td>Affects one component's accents</td>
    </tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

---

## Using Themes in Storybook

Storybook provides three places to control themes:

### Top Toolbar (Global Controls)

Located at the very top of Storybook:

- **Site: Global Theme** - Changes the site-wide color palette for all components
- **Typography: Heading Fonts** - Switches between YaleNew and Mallory heading fonts

These controls apply to ALL stories and persist as you navigate.

### Controls Panel (Story-Specific)

Located at the bottom of each story:

- **Section Theme** - Controls the background color for that specific story
- **[Component] Theme (dial)** - Controls the accent color for the component in that story

These controls only affect the current story.

### VRT Sections (Visual Regression Testing)

Many stories include automated VRT sections showing all theme combinations. These are static and used for Percy visual regression testing.

---

## Best Practices

### For Content Editors

1. **Start with Global Theme**: Choose your site's global theme first - this is typically set once and rarely changed
2. **Use Section Themes Sparingly**: Too many colored backgrounds can be overwhelming. Use `default` (white) for most sections
3. **Add Component Themes for Accent**: Use component themes to highlight important elements or create visual interest
4. **Test Contrast**: Always check that text is readable against your chosen backgrounds

### For Developers

1. **Respect the Hierarchy**: Don't fight the cascade - work with the three-tier system
2. **Use Theme Tokens**: Always use theme-aware CSS custom properties, never hard-coded colors
3. **Test All Combinations**: Use Storybook's VRT sections to verify components work across all theme combinations
4. **Document Theme Usage**: When creating new components, document which theme types they support

### For Designers

1. **Design with Themes in Mind**: Create components that work across all global theme palettes
2. **Consider Context**: Remember components may appear on different section backgrounds
3. **Maintain Contrast**: Ensure sufficient contrast ratios across all theme combinations (WCAG AA minimum)
4. **Use Storybook**: Review components in Storybook across different theme combinations before finalizing designs

---

## Common Questions

**Q: Can I mix and match themes however I want?**
A: Yes! The system is designed to work across all combinations. However, use restraint - too many theme variations on one page can be visually overwhelming.

**Q: What happens if I don't set a theme?**
A: Components will use the `default` option, which respects the global theme palette.

**Q: Can I add custom themes?**
A: Global themes are defined in the design tokens and require coordination with the design system team. Section and component themes use the same six options across the system.

**Q: Why can't I change component themes in the top toolbar?**
A: Component themes are intentionally component-specific (not global) to allow fine-grained control. Each component can have its own accent color.
